jokes.text: When does that come out? / I don't know.
jokes.setup: Larry asks about movie release date
jokes.punchline: George doesn't know when his own movie comes out
jokes.explanation: Sets up the running pattern of George not knowing his own release dates

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: When does that come out?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't know.

jokes.text: 'And has she been released?' 'No. She's still in the can.'
jokes.setup: Discussion about selling house and Mrs. Segal
jokes.punchline: Double meaning of 'in the can' - prison vs. film/TV production
jokes.explanation: Perfect wordplay with elegant misdirection - audience thinks one thing, reality is another. Economical and surprising.

dialogue.line: And has she been released?
No. She's still in the can.

jokes.text: Artie: 'I almost had to run to the can myself' after praising the 'in the can' joke
jokes.setup: Artie commenting on the previous 'in the can' exchange
jokes.punchline: Uses 'can' in yet another meaning (bathroom) while referencing the joke
jokes.explanation: Smart callback that extends the wordplay while staying in character for Artie

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I almost had to run to the can myself

jokes.text: 'But we have him on every month, Artie.' 'Hank's on every night.' 'I've been meaning to talk to you about that.'
jokes.setup: Larry complaining about booking George Segal too often
jokes.punchline: Artie's deadpan comparison to Hank being on every night, Larry's matter-of-fact response
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Artie's dry wit and Larry's oblivious self-centeredness. Great structure and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: But we have him on every month, Artie.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Hank's on every night.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I've been meaning to talk to you about that.

jokes.text: Artie reading the fall schedule: 'Ben vereen. Up and tapping, thank god. Susan dey. Pertful little skirtfull.'
jokes.punchline: Artie's increasingly sarcastic and inappropriate commentary
jokes.explanation: Pure Artie - inappropriate, world-weary, and perfectly delivered. 'Pertful little skirtfull' is inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Ben vereen. Up and tapping, thank god. Susan dey. Pertful little skirtfull.

jokes.text: 'That's why the good lord invented videotape.'
jokes.setup: Larry can't remember what he says to guests
jokes.punchline: Artie's dry solution to Larry's existential crisis
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie - practical and dismissive of Larry's neuroses with perfect deadpan delivery

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: That's why the good lord invented videotape.

jokes.text: '10:00 tomorrow!' 'That means 10:30?' 'Yeah. 10:30 sharp.'
jokes.punchline: The automatic translation of Larry's stated time to what it actually means
jokes.explanation: Perfect workplace dynamic - everyone knows Larry's times don't mean what they say. Great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: 10:00 tomorrow!

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: That means 10:30?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Yeah. 10:30 sharp.

jokes.text: Hank's dramatic prediction: 'Mark my words, children. Somebody is going down. They started with Jerry. They'll do it again.'
jokes.punchline: Hank's over-dramatic prophecy of doom
jokes.explanation: Pure Hank - dramatic, self-important, and completely over the top for the situation

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Mark my words, children. Somebody is going down. They started with Jerry. They'll do it again.

jokes.text: 'We didn't introduce R.E.M. as "rem" last week.' 'Oh, my brave, brave Phil. Always with the jokes and yet so very desperate.'
jokes.setup: Phil trying to deflect firing fears with humor
jokes.punchline: Hank's brutal psychological read of Phil's defense mechanism
jokes.explanation: Hank at his most perceptive and cruel. Perfect character insight delivered with theatrical flair.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: We didn't introduce R.E.M. as "rem" last week.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, my brave, brave Phil. Always with the jokes and yet so very desperate.

jokes.text: 'If somebody gets fired, it will be me.' 'No. No, it is. I've been here the shortest time.' Multiple people claiming they'll be fired
jokes.punchline: Everyone desperately trying to predict their own doom
jokes.explanation: Good workplace anxiety escalation, though fairly standard for the genre

dialogue.line: If somebody gets fired, it will be me.
No. No, it is. I've been here the shortest time.

jokes.text: 'He'll take antidepressants, and everything will be normal.'
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of antidepressants as the solution to show problems
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about showbiz mental health, delivered matter-of-factly

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: He'll take antidepressants, and everything will be normal.

jokes.text: 'I'm just getting a haircut.' Larry sitting in barber chair during his own crisis meeting
jokes.punchline: Larry is getting his hair cut during the meeting he called
jokes.explanation: Perfect visual representation of Larry's priorities - vanity over leadership. Absurd but completely in character.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm just getting a haircut.

jokes.text: 'Hey, a haircut, huh?' 'No. He's just checking it.' 'That's a great haircut.' 'He's just started.'
jokes.punchline: The haircut hasn't actually started yet but people are already complimenting it
jokes.explanation: Absurd workplace sycophancy - people complimenting something that hasn't happened yet

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Hey, a haircut, huh?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No. He's just checking it.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: That's a great haircut.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: He's just started.

jokes.text: 'Do you want to just give me one second?' Larry interrupting his own urgent meeting for haircut maintenance
jokes.setup: Larry starting to address the serious problem
jokes.punchline: Immediately pausing for hair primping
jokes.explanation: Peak Larry narcissism - can't focus on work crisis because of hair concerns

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Do you want to just give me one second?

jokes.text: 'I can sum up with one word-- guests.' 'Guests. Thank you, Jesus.' 'What did you think the problem was?' '[All] Guests.'
jokes.setup: Big buildup to reveal the serious problem
jokes.punchline: It's just booking guests, and everyone already knew
jokes.explanation: Good anticlimax - all the buildup for something obvious everyone already knew

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I can sum up with one word-- guests.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Guests. Thank you, Jesus.
What did you think the problem was?
[All] Guests.

jokes.text: 'I know you, Kiki. How's your... How's your, uh--' 'Well. Thanks.'
jokes.setup: Larry trying to make personal connection with staff member
jokes.punchline: Can't remember anything about her life, trails off awkwardly
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry social awkwardness - pretending to care but clearly clueless

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I know you, Kiki. How's your... How's your, uh--

dialogue.speaker: Kiki
dialogue.line: Well. Thanks.

jokes.text: 'Could you repeat that last part?' 'Which part?' 'About the people...' 'Was I unclear about--' 'Clear to me.' 'Perfectly clear.'
jokes.punchline: Immediate backtracking when Larry gets defensive
jokes.explanation: Good workplace power dynamic - staff member immediately backs down from reasonable request

dialogue.line: Could you repeat that last part?
Which part?
About the people...
Was I unclear about--
Clear to me.
Perfectly clear.

jokes.text: Could you repeat that last part? Which part? About the people... Was I unclear about-- Clear to me. Perfectly clear.
jokes.setup: Larry gives direction about finding new guests
jokes.punchline: Hank wasn't listening, creates awkward back-and-forth
jokes.explanation: Classic workplace awkwardness - Hank's inattention creates uncomfortable exchange.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Could you repeat that last part?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Which part?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: About the people...

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Was I unclear about--

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Clear to me. Perfectly clear.

jokes.text: 'And who is Al Nelson?' 'He's the plate spinner.' 'He was a regular on Sullivan 30 years ago.'
jokes.punchline: Booking a plate spinner from 30 years ago
jokes.explanation: Good example of stale TV booking - relying on ancient variety show acts

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: And who is Al Nelson?
He's the plate spinner.
He was a regular on Sullivan 30 years ago.

jokes.text: 'He's older now, and he's started using Chinette, but people still flip for him.'
jokes.punchline: Now uses paper plates instead of real ones, but it's still exciting
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist detail - the degradation from real plates to paper plates perfectly captures faded showbiz glory

dialogue.line: He's older now, and he's started using Chinette, but people still flip for him.

jokes.text: 'This is a booking problem, right?' '[Artie] Phil, shut the fuck up.' 'I'll handle it. Phil, shut the fuck up.'
jokes.punchline: Both Artie and Larry simultaneously telling him to shut up
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with both bosses ganging up on poor Phil in unison

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: This is a booking problem, right?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Phil, shut the fuck up.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'll handle it. Phil, shut the fuck up.

jokes.text: 'Mike, you're new here.' 'I'll let that slide, but--' 'Hank, put your hand down.'
jokes.setup: Larry asking for input from the new guy
jokes.punchline: Hank immediately raising his hand like he's in school
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank behavior - treating a meeting like elementary school and being completely oblivious

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Mike, you're new here.

dialogue.speaker: Mike
dialogue.line: I'll let that slide, but--

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Hank, put your hand down.

jokes.text: 'Robert Conrad.' 'No. No. Owes me a favor. Pick up the phone. Hello, Bobby.'
jokes.punchline: Immediately role-playing the phone call to his 'friend' Robert Conrad
jokes.explanation: Pure Hank delusion - pretending to have celebrity connections and acting it out

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Robert Conrad.
No. No. Owes me a favor. Pick up the phone. Hello, Bobby.

jokes.text: 'Raymond Burr, with whom I once had the honor of playing golf.' 'Hello, Perry.'
jokes.punchline: Again immediately pretending to call him, using his character name
jokes.explanation: Continuation of Hank's delusional celebrity connections, calling Raymond Burr by his character name Perry Mason

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Raymond Burr, with whom I once had the honor of playing golf.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Hello, Perry.

jokes.text: 'Performance art. That's a bunch of dog poop. What does he do? Get stark naked? Cover himself with chocolate syrup?'
jokes.setup: Discussing booking performance artist Tim Miller
jokes.punchline: Artie's crude dismissal and stereotypical assumptions
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie - old-school entertainment industry dismissal of anything avant-garde

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Performance art. That's a bunch of dog poop. What does he do? Get stark naked? Cover himself with chocolate syrup?

jokes.text: Reading the pretentious review: 'A garrulous assault of anecdote, humor, observation, and social comment punctuated by movement and channeled through life experience.'
jokes.setup: Larry asking if the performance artist is funny
jokes.punchline: Reading overwrought art criticism that says nothing
jokes.explanation: Good satire of pretentious art criticism - lots of words that mean nothing

dialogue.line: Reading the pretentious review: 'A garrulous assault of anecdote, humor, observation, and social comment punctuated by movement and channeled through life experience.'

jokes.text: A sperm looking like Jesse Helms tries to catch me. I elude him and find a willing lesbian ovum.
jokes.punchline: Political figure as evil sperm, lesbian egg as ally
jokes.explanation: Brilliant political satire embedded in biological absurdism - Jesse Helms as villain sperm is inspired.

dialogue.line: A sperm looking like Jesse Helms tries to catch me. I elude him and find a willing lesbian ovum.

jokes.text: 'All right. All right. That is not necessary.' Larry's awkward attempt to control the booing
jokes.punchline: Larry's weak, ineffective attempt at damage control
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry response - trying to be the good guy but completely out of his depth

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: All right. All right. That is not necessary.

jokes.text: 'I apologize for our audience. Let's try and stay open.' '[Hank] Right. Stay open.'
jokes.setup: Larry trying to be progressive and understanding
jokes.punchline: Hank immediately parroting Larry's sentiment without understanding
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - mindlessly echoing Larry without any genuine understanding or conviction

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I apologize for our audience. Let's try and stay open.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Right. Stay open.

jokes.text: 'Go back to San Francisco!' 'I'm from L.A.' Basic geographic correction
jokes.punchline: Tim's matter-of-fact correction of the wrong assumption
jokes.explanation: Great deadpan response - deflating the bigot's assumption with simple facts

dialogue.speaker: Audience member
dialogue.line: Go back to San Francisco!

dialogue.speaker: Tim Miller
dialogue.line: I'm from L.A.

jokes.text: I warned you with my eyes. I heard nothing. I saw nothing.
jokes.setup: Larry blames Artie for not warning him about the performance content
jokes.punchline: Artie claims he warned with eye contact, Larry pleads ignorance
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist exchange - the idea of warning 'with eyes' and Larry's Sergeant Schultz defense.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I warned you with my eyes.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I heard nothing. I saw nothing.

jokes.text: 'The tape was not that gay.' Larry's confused damage assessment
jokes.setup: Larry trying to understand what went wrong
jokes.punchline: Measuring 'gayness' levels like it's a technical specification
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Larry logic - trying to quantify something unquantifiable in the most awkward way possible

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: The tape was not that gay.

jokes.text: 'He's talking about butt plugs. He's talking about his father's orgasm.'
jokes.setup: Larry explaining why the performance was problematic
jokes.punchline: List of increasingly uncomfortable topics
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of discomfort, showing Larry's specific concerns

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: He's talking about butt plugs. He's talking about his father's orgasm.

jokes.text: 'We think you're terrific, Timbo.' immediately followed by Tim asking not to be called Timbo
jokes.setup: Artie trying to smooth things over
jokes.punchline: Immediately offending with the condescending nickname
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie move - trying to be friendly but immediately making it worse with inappropriate familiarity

jokes.text: 'Listen to me, Melody, the man said "butt plug" three times! And you and I both know you can't--' 'You can say "butt plug"? Since when?'
jokes.setup: Artie arguing with network about standards
jokes.punchline: Discovering the network actually allows the thing he's complaining about
jokes.explanation: Great reversal - Artie's assumptions about censorship are wrong, undermining his entire argument

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Listen to me, Melody, the man said "butt plug" three times! And you and I both know you can't--
You can say "butt plug"? Since when?

jokes.text: 'The network says that it is our decision and they find no problem with the piece.' 'What do you mean?' 'They're hanging us out to dry.'
jokes.punchline: Artie recognizing this as corporate cowardice, not support
jokes.explanation: Smart industry insight - when networks give you 'freedom,' they're really giving you rope to hang yourself

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: The network says that it is our decision and they find no problem with the piece.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: They're hanging us out to dry.

jokes.text: You can say 'butt plug'? Since when?
jokes.punchline: Artie's incredulous question about broadcast standards
jokes.explanation: Artie's confusion about shifting broadcast standards, plays on the absurdity of censorship rules

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You can say 'butt plug'? Since when?

jokes.text: 'I'll count to 10, and if you agree with my choice, don't say anything.' 'I agree.' 'Then don't say anything.' 'I got excited. I had the answer.'
jokes.setup: Artie setting up a system for Larry to participate in the decision
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately breaking the system because he's excited to agree
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry moment - can't even follow simple instructions because of his need to participate and please

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I'll count to 10, and if you agree with my choice, don't say anything.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I agree.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Then don't say anything.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I got excited. I had the answer.

jokes.text: I agree. Then don't say anything. I got excited. I had the answer.
jokes.setup: Larry supposed to stay quiet to show agreement
jokes.punchline: Larry can't help but speak because he's excited about knowing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry character moment - his childlike need for approval overrides simple instructions.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I agree.
Then don't say anything.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I got excited. I had the answer.

jokes.text: 'I told my friends to watch Tim Miller last night.' 'Why, Phil?' 'They haven't seen anything that good since my college roommate's nervous breakdown.'
jokes.punchline: Comparing it to a mental health crisis as entertainment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor - perfectly captures Phil's twisted sense of entertainment value

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: I told my friends to watch Tim Miller last night.
Why, Phil?

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: They haven't seen anything that good since my college roommate's nervous breakdown.

jokes.text: Liz Smith review: 'Sanders cans performance by Miller. Late-night funny man Larry Sanders couldn't take the heat last night, so he threw Mr. Miller out of the kitchen.'
jokes.punchline: Mixing metaphors in the most clichéd way possible
jokes.explanation: Good satire of gossip column writing - mixing metaphors and clichés

dialogue.line: Liz Smith review: 'Sanders cans performance by Miller. Late-night funny man Larry Sanders couldn't take the heat last night, so he threw Mr. Miller out of the kitchen.'

jokes.text: 'Calling him the Jesse Helms of late-night television.' 'Well, hell. That's not so bad.'
jokes.setup: Reading harsh criticism comparing Larry to conservative senator
jokes.punchline: Artie thinking being compared to Jesse Helms isn't that bad
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie response - completely missing why being compared to Jesse Helms is bad

dialogue.speaker: Staff
dialogue.line: Calling him the Jesse Helms of late-night television.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Well, hell. That's not so bad.

jokes.text: 'I'm not mentioned in either article.' Hank's hurt feelings about being left out of controversy
jokes.punchline: Hank's primary concern is his own omission from the story
jokes.explanation: Peak Hank narcissism - in the middle of a crisis, he's upset about not getting press attention

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I'm not mentioned in either article.

jokes.text: 'Your brain doesn't have any cognizance of anything.' Larry's attempt at profound criticism
jokes.punchline: Using 'cognizance' awkwardly to sound smart
jokes.explanation: Good Larry moment - trying to sound intellectual but using words awkwardly

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Your brain doesn't have any cognizance of anything.

jokes.text: And my, uh, whole face. It looks great. Your whole face.
jokes.setup: Larry admitting to plastic surgery piece by piece
jokes.punchline: Final admission that he had his entire face done
jokes.explanation: Good escalation gag with Larry's vanity leading to total facial reconstruction admission.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: And my, uh, whole face. It looks great.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Your whole face.

jokes.text: 'If you're so square, I might as well do Leno.' '[Audience] Ooh!'
jokes.punchline: Threatening to go to the competition, audience reacts
jokes.explanation: Good industry burn - threatening to go to the competition hits where it hurts

dialogue.speaker: Guest comedian
dialogue.line: If you're so square, I might as well do Leno.

dialogue.speaker: Audience
dialogue.line: Ooh!

jokes.text: 'I hide them behind a desk.' Continuing the testicle joke
jokes.punchline: Visual of hiding behind the desk for protection
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up that extends the visual metaphor

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I hide them behind a desk.

jokes.text: 'And don't call me Timbo anymore, okay?' Tim Miller's irritated request
jokes.punchline: Direct confrontation about the disrespectful name
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier condescension, showing Tim's growing frustration

dialogue.speaker: Tim Miller
dialogue.line: And don't call me Timbo anymore, okay?

jokes.text: 'Television degrades everybody on it and everybody who watches it.'
jokes.punchline: Broad condemnation of the entire medium
jokes.explanation: Meta-commentary on the show itself and the medium - biting the hand that feeds

dialogue.speaker: Tim Miller
dialogue.line: Television degrades everybody on it and everybody who watches it.

jokes.text: Tim Miller performing the same material on a competing show and being celebrated
jokes.punchline: Another show airs it without problems and celebrates it
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic reversal - what Larry couldn't handle becomes a triumph elsewhere, highlighting his cowardice

jokes.text: Turn it off. You know, Tim's great. He's got a story to tell. He's telling how we screwed him.
jokes.setup: Watching Tim Miller's appearance on another show
jokes.punchline: Larry acknowledging Tim is good while watching him trash their show
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic moment - Larry forced to acknowledge Tim's talent while being publicly humiliated.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Turn it off. You know, Tim's great. He's got a story to tell. He's telling how we screwed him.

jokes.text: 'There's this comic I saw on the Leno show-- a guy named Tim Miller-- who would have been sensational. He's the kind of guy who'd be perfect on this show. You should book him.'
jokes.setup: Guest praising Tim Miller without knowing the history
jokes.punchline: Unknowingly recommending the exact person Larry just censored
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending - the ultimate ironic twist that Tim Miller is now being recommended to Larry as the perfect guest. Brilliant full-circle structure.

dialogue.speaker: Guest
dialogue.line: There's this comic I saw on the Leno show-- a guy named Tim Miller-- who would have been sensational. He's the kind of guy who'd be perfect on this show. You should book him.
